reasoning:
The CBOE Gold ETF Volatility Index (GVZ) recently experienced a sudden spike, increasing from 15.09 on August 25 to 17.39 on August 29. Volatility indices typically strongly exhibit mean-reverting behavior, meaning sharp short-term spikes often correct downward soon after, especially when not tied to major new systemic shocks. The FRED statistical analysis suggests a 29% probability that the index will remain above 17.39 by September 7, capturing this mean-reversion tendency. 

The historical data provided for GVZ over the past year shows values ranging between 14.62 and 24.53, with values above 17.39 occurring less than half the time (about 35-40% of the sampled dates). Since the index spent most of July and August 2025 in the 15-16 range and only breached 17 due to very recent momentum, I strongly expect the short-term value (Sep 7 and 30) to fall below the 17.39 high-water mark. Looking further ahead, uncertainty naturally increases and the probability regresses toward the unconditional base rate of GVZ exceeding 17.39, which appears to be somewhat under 50% given the historical distribution. Therefore, probabilities increase with the time horizon, ending near 0.44 for February 2026.
